text_blocks.text: In cPDNA, there are several A - G
deamination gradients. DNA becomes susceptible to
deamination events when it is single stranded. When
replication forks form, the strand not being copied
is single stranded, and thus at risk for A - G
indicate that replication forks were most likely
present and the direction that they initially opened
(the highest gradient is most likely nearest the
start site because it was single stranded for the
longest amount of time). This mechanism is still the
leading theory today; however, a second theory
suggests that most cPDNA is actually linear and
further contends that only a minority of the
genetic material is kept in circular chromosomes
while the rest is in branched, linear, or other
